sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, is a 68-year-old male presenting with a non-healing sore on his right foot that has been present for approximately 2-3 weeks.
He reports that he initially had a blister from a new pair of shoes, but the wound has not improved despite treatment with antibiotics.
He denies any trauma to the foot and reports no pain or discomfort in the affected area.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes, with an A1C of over 8% on his last check.
He also mentions having a podiatrist in the past, but they are no longer available.
Review of systems: The patient denies any fever, chills, drainage from the wound, or other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a diabetic foot ulcer that requires further evaluation and treatment.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected vascular insufficiency; suspected diabetic neuropathy; diabetes mellitus; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I ordered an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the patient's foot.
I performed a debridement to remove dead tissue from the wound.
I prescribed a collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
The patient is to wear a surgical shoe to reduce pressure on the affected area.
I do not think additional antibiotics are necessary at this time.
The patient is to return in two weeks for follow-up, and we may refer him to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates inadequate blood supply for wound healing.
Follow-up: The patient is to return in two weeks for follow-up.
